title: Indonesian Nasi Goreng

ingredients.name: day-old cooked rice
visual_checks: cold leftover rice fries best — fresh rice turns mushy
ingredients.notes: cold leftover rice fries best — fresh rice turns mushy

ingredients.name: eggs
visual_checks: 2 for scrambling into the rice, 1 fried on top per serving
ingredients.notes: 2 for scrambling into the rice, 1 fried on top per serving

subs.name: silken tofu scrambled in 1 tbsp oil for a vegan version
150 g diced firm tofu, shallow-fried until golden

ingredients.name: dark soy sauce (or kecap manis)
visual_checks: kecap manis is sweet Indonesian soy; mix 1 tbsp dark soy + 1 tsp jaggery if unavailable
ingredients.notes: kecap manis is sweet Indonesian soy; mix 1 tbsp dark soy + 1 tsp jaggery if unavailable

subs.name: mix 1 tbsp soy sauce + 1 tsp jaggery to mimic sweet kecap manis
1 extra tsp soy sauce plus a squeeze of lemon

steps.text: If using prawn, toss them with a pinch of salt and red chilli powder. Set aside. Beat 2 of the eggs in a bowl and season lightly with salt.
Heat 1 tbsp oil in a wok or kadai on high flame. Add the prawn and stir-fry for 1–2 minutes until pink and just cooked through. Remove and set aside.

Add the remaining 2 tbsp oil to the same wok on high heat. Add garlic and green chilli and stir for 20 seconds until fragrant, then add the onion slices and stir-fry for 1–2 minutes until the edges colour.
Add the cold rice and press it into the wok with a spatula. Toss and press repeatedly for 2–3 minutes, breaking any lumps, until every grain is coated and the rice smells toasty.
Push the rice to the sides. Pour the beaten egg into the centre and let it set for 20 seconds, then scramble and fold it through the rice quickly before it dries out.

Return the prawn to the wok. Add soy sauce, fish sauce and remaining red chilli powder. Toss on high heat for 1 minute until everything is evenly coated and glossy. Check salt — soy sauce is already salty.
Drizzle sesame oil and scatter half the spring onions. Divide into bowls. In the same pan, fry the remaining egg sunny-side up in a little oil and perch one on each serving.
Garnish with remaining spring onions and serve immediately — nasi goreng waits for no one once it comes off the heat.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish-sauce → 1 extra tsp soy sauce plus a squeeze of lemon (makes it pescatarian-free); prawn → 150 g diced firm tofu, shallow-fried until golden (makes it vegetarian); soy-sauce → mix 1 tbsp soy sauce + 1 tsp jaggery to mimic sweet kecap manis (closer to the street-stall taste); egg → silken tofu scrambled in 1 tbsp oil for a vegan version
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use a kadai on the highest flame your hob will give — the best substitute for a wok. Cold leftover sona masoori or ponni rice works perfectly. Replace kecap manis with a mix of 1 tbsp dark soy sauce and 1 tsp jaggery (gur). Fish sauce is now available at Big Basket or Natures Basket; skip it or use a dash more soy. Sesame oil (til ka tel) is available at most Indian grocers.
